test_id_1.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, and Medicaid
Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.
Through monitoring real-time claims, NYRx has identified two trends in pharmacy submitted 340B
drug claims:
1.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.answers: {"headline_summary":"Current provider-manual guidance says full-benefit dual eligible members must obtain Medicare Part D-covered drugs through their Part D plan, with Medicaid generally not covering Part D drug classes. The policy evidence partially aligns because it says NYRx covers only limited listed drugs for dual eligibles and that other prescription drugs should be billed to Part D, but it does not directly address the provider-manual payment-limit and cost-sharing rule.","aligned_pairs":[{"provider_manual":"For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members must use their Part D plan for those drugs, and members and prescribers may need to use the plan's exception and appeals process for needed nonformulary prescriptions. The manual also states that combined Medicare and Medicaid payment cannot exceed the Medicaid-only amount, and pharmacies cannot collect Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicare and Medicaid dually eligible individuals.","policy_update":"For dual eligible New York State Medicaid members enrolled in a Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug plan, NYRx covers only specified listed drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan. The policy is more service-specific because it names examples such as certain vitamins, cough and cold products, benzonatate, and excludes compounded prescriptions for dual eligibles."},{"provider_manual":"Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required to submit claims and accept assignment for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members. This applies regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies must determine whether CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment, or both, is required for their business model.","policy_update":"The policy update routes most dual eligible prescription coverage through the member's Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug plan, except for limited NYRx-covered drugs. However, it does not directly discuss pharmacy Medicare enrollment, claim submission obligations, or assignment requirements. This is therefore only a partial match on the same operational topic."}],"similarities":["Both sources direct most dual eligible prescription drug coverage away from Medicaid and toward the member's Medicare drug coverage, with only limited Medicaid-covered exceptions on the policy side [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4]."],"differences":["The provider manual adds payment and provider-duty rules, including the Medicaid-only payment ceiling, payment-in-full requirement, and mandatory claim submission and assignment for Medicare-covered services, while the policy update focuses on which dual eligible drugs remain covered by NYRx and does not state those operational requirements [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4]."],"caveats":"The policy evidence is only a partial match for the provider-manual guidance because the retrieved policy chunk does not directly address the manual's Medicare payment-limit, cost-sharing, or pharmacy enrollment and assignment rules."}

test_id_2.answers: {"headline_summary":"Current provider-manual guidance says dual eligible pharmacy claims for Medicare Part D-covered drugs should go through the member's Part D plan, not Medicaid, and it also adds Medicare and Medicaid payment-limit and cost-sharing rules. The policy evidence supports the same core billing pathway but is narrower and older because it focuses on limited NYRx-covered drug exceptions for dual eligibles rather than the full payment and provider-obligation framework.","aligned_pairs":[{"provider_manual":"For full-benefit dual eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members must obtain those drugs through their Part D plan. Members and prescribers may need to use the Part D plan's exception and appeals process for necessary nonformulary prescriptions.","policy_update":"For dual eligible members enrolled in a Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug plan, NYRx covers only specified listed drugs. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan. The policy is narrower because it highlights examples such as certain vitamins, cough and cold products, benzonatate, and no coverage of compounded prescriptions for dual eligibles."},{"provider_manual":"Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers must submit claims and accept assignment for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies must determine whether their business model requires CMS-855B, CMS-855S, or both Medicare enrollments.","policy_update":"The policy update also routes most dual eligible prescription drug claims to the Part D drug plan, except for limited NYRx-covered drugs. However, it does not directly address provider claim-submission duties, assignment, or Medicare enrollment requirements. This is only a partial match for the provider-manual topic."}],"similarities":["Both sources say that most dual eligible prescription drug claims should go through the member's Medicare drug coverage rather than Medicaid, with only limited Medicaid or NYRx exceptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4]."],"differences":["The provider manual adds payment-in-full, Medicaid-only payment-limit, Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, and pharmacy billing-obligation rules, while the policy update mainly lists the dual eligible drugs that NYRx still covers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4]."],"caveats":"The policy evidence is only a partial match overall because the retrieved policy chunk does not directly address the provider manual's Medicare and Medicaid payment-limit, cost-sharing, or pharmacy enrollment and assignment requirements."}

amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.

https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

test_id_5.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.

policy.text:
Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims, as outlined in the NYRx
the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://

Update articles. All NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims (when NYS Medicaid is primary payor)
must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost. Providers should refer to the
NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines, located at: https://
www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf, for requirements
when NYS Medicaid is secondary payor. Claim level identifiers are required on eligible 340B purchased drug
claims for NYS Medicaid members.

drug claims:
1.

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.

test_id_6.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Those claims must go through the member's Part D plan. If a needed drug is not on the formulary, the member and prescriber must use the Part D plan's exception and appeals process.","policy_update":"Policy guidance says any responsible third party must pay before Medicaid when other coverage is available. For pharmacy claims, members with other coverage will deny at point of service with NCPDP response code 717, \"Client Has Other Insurance.\" This policy is broader and less specific because it states the general other-coverage-first rule rather than a dual-eligible Part D rule."}],"similarities":["Both sources place primary payer responsibility on non-Medicaid coverage before Medicaid pays for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13]."],"differences":["The provider manual specifically assigns dual-eligible Part D drug claims to the member's Medicare Part D plan, while the policy source states a general third-party-liability rule and point-of-service denial pathway without naming dual-eligible Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13]."],"caveats":"The policy source is an older and broader billing reminder, so it only partially matches the provider manual's dual-eligible pharmacy rule."}

.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.query_content: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of
both Medicare and NYS Medicaid), NYS Medicaid members with third-party health insurance (TPHI), or
individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Since co-payments paid or incurred can be used toward satisfying the spend-down (overage)
in the following month, itemized bills or receipts for co-payments should be provided to members when
requested.
• There is a maximum amount per Medicaid member for all co-payments incurred per year and is calculated
on a quarterly basis. The co-payment year starts April 1 and ends March 31. When a member reaches the
quarterly co-pay maximum, they will receive a letter confirming the date on which the co-pay maximum
was met and exempting the member from a co-payment until the end of the current co-payment quarter.
• Co-payment amounts are as follows:
o $3.00 for non-preferred Brand Name Drugs.
o $1.00 for Generic Drugs, preferred Brand Name Drugs, and Brand Drugs included in the Brand Less
Than Generic Drugs Program.
o $0.50 for Non-Prescription (over the counter) Drugs.
o $1.00 for Medical/Sickroom Supplies.
• Co-payment is not required for certain members and service categories which include:

test_id_12.query_content: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of
both Medicare and NYS Medicaid), NYS Medicaid members with third-party health insurance (TPHI), or
individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Since co-payments paid or incurred can be used toward satisfying the spend-down (overage)
in the following month, itemized bills or receipts for co-payments should be provided to members when
requested.
• There is a maximum amount per Medicaid member for all co-payments incurred per year and is calculated
on a quarterly basis. The co-payment year starts April 1 and ends March 31. When a member reaches the
quarterly co-pay maximum, they will receive a letter confirming the date on which the co-pay maximum
was met and exempting the member from a co-payment until the end of the current co-payment quarter.
• Co-payment amounts are as follows:
o $3.00 for non-preferred Brand Name Drugs.
o $1.00 for Generic Drugs, preferred Brand Name Drugs, and Brand Drugs included in the Brand Less
Than Generic Drugs Program.
o $0.50 for Non-Prescription (over the counter) Drugs.
o $1.00 for Medical/Sickroom Supplies.
• Co-payment is not required for certain members and service categories which include:

o Family planning (birth control) services including birth control pills, emergency contraception, and
condoms.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

• Co-payment is not required for certain members and service categories which include:

o Family planning (birth control) services including birth control pills, emergency contraception, and
condoms.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.

test_id_15.answers: {"headline_summary":"The provider manual gives the clearest current guidance: for dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, as payment in full, and it cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The policy evidence is directionally similar on dual-eligible balance-billing protection, but it is less pharmacy-specific and does not directly address standard Medicaid pharmacy co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.","aligned_pairs":[{"provider_manual":"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must accept Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, as payment in full. A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance from the member. The manual also states that no additional payment is made if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee.","policy_update":"For Medicare Part C benefits, the provider must accept the Medicare Advantage plan payment and any Medicaid payment as payment in full. The provider cannot bill the dual-eligible member for unreimbursed Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amounts. This is only a partial match because it addresses Part C cost sharing rather than the pharmacy manual's Part B-focused rule."},{"provider_manual":"The provider manual sets standard Medicaid pharmacy co-payment amounts and a quarterly co-pay maximum. The retrieved chunk lists examples such as $3.00 for non-preferred brand name drugs and $1.00 for generic drugs. However, this chunk does not identify dual-eligible members as a specific exemption category.","policy_update":"The retrieved policy chunk says incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members. It does not address pharmacy co-payment responsibility or any dual-eligible co-pay exemption. This is therefore only a partial match and mainly shows a gap in the retrieved policy evidence."}],"similarities":["Both sources support protection against billing dual-eligible members for certain unreimbursed Medicare-related cost-sharing amounts when Medicare and Medicaid both apply [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5]."],"differences":["The provider manual gives a pharmacy-specific rule on payment in full and bars recovery of Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance, while the policy source addresses Medicare Part C cost sharing and does not directly state a dual-eligible pharmacy co-payment rule [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5, New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11]."],"caveats":"No retrieved policy chunk directly addresses standard Medicaid pharmacy co-payment responsibility or a dual-eligible co-pay exemption, so the policy comparison is partial."}

test_id_16.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy
Drug Coverage for New York State Medicaid Dual Eligible Members
Effective October 22, 2020, Medicaid will implement claims editing that will ensure over-the-counter (OTC)
insulin and legend drugs with available OTC drug substitutes are appropriately billed to Medicare Part D
prescription drug plans and Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) plans.
As previously communicated in the December 2013 and June 2014 Medicaid Update articles titled “Change
in Coverage of Barbiturates for Dual Eligible Population” and “Update on Drug Coverage for the Dual Eligible
Population” respectively, coverage is available for select prescription vitamins and OTC drugs for New
York State (NYS) Medicaid dual eligible members. Only Medicaid-reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare
are covered for dual eligible members. Therefore, NYS Medicaid will no longer cover OTC insulin and some
OTC products which have legend drug substitutes that are covered by Medicare Part D and MAPD plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2020 Volume 36 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_16.answers: {"headline_summary":"Current provider-manual guidance says Medicaid generally should not be billed for a Medicare Part D member’s drug unless the drug class is specifically excluded from Part D by law. The older policy guidance is broadly consistent, but it adds a specific 2020 limit that OTC insulin and some OTC products with legend substitutes must be billed to Medicare Part D or MAPD instead.","aligned_pairs":[{"provider_manual":"Medicaid should not be billed for a Medicare Part D member’s prescription drug unless the drug class is specifically excluded from Part D by law. For dual-eligible members, Medicaid pays only for drugs on the Medicaid reimbursable list in those excluded classes, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. Those Medicaid claims remain subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.","policy_update":"Policy says only Medicaid-reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare are covered for dual-eligible members. It gives examples including select prescription vitamins and OTC drugs. It also explains that some items must instead be billed to Medicare Part D or MAPD."},{"provider_manual":"For full-benefit dual-eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plan’s formulary, exceptions, and appeals process when a needed prescription is not covered on the formulary. This is the current general rule for routing covered drug classes away from Medicaid.","policy_update":"Policy implemented claims editing so OTC insulin and some OTC products with available legend drug substitutes are no longer covered by Medicaid. Those products must be billed to Medicare Part D or MAPD instead. This is a partial match because it gives a narrower 2020 product-specific example rather than the provider manual’s broader current rule."}],"similarities":["Both sources say Medicaid may cover only Medicare-excluded drugs that are also Medicaid-reimbursable for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2020 Volume 36 Number 13]."],"differences":["The provider manual states a broader current rule that Medicaid does not cover any drug class covered under Part D for full-benefit dual eligibles, while the policy source adds a specific 2020 operational limit for OTC insulin and some OTC products with legend substitutes that must go to Part D or MAPD [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2020 Volume 36 Number 13]."],"caveats":"The policy comparison is partial for products with Part D-covered substitutes because the retrieved policy source gives a narrower 2020 claims-editing example rather than a general current rule."}

https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
August 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy
Drug Coverage for New York State Medicaid Dual Eligible Members
Effective October 22, 2020, Medicaid will implement claims editing that will ensure over-the-counter (OTC)
insulin and legend drugs with available OTC drug substitutes are appropriately billed to Medicare Part D
prescription drug plans and Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) plans.
As previously communicated in the December 2013 and June 2014 Medicaid Update articles titled “Change
in Coverage of Barbiturates for Dual Eligible Population” and “Update on Drug Coverage for the Dual Eligible
Population” respectively, coverage is available for select prescription vitamins and OTC drugs for New
York State (NYS) Medicaid dual eligible members. Only Medicaid-reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare
are covered for dual eligible members. Therefore, NYS Medicaid will no longer cover OTC insulin and some
OTC products which have legend drug substitutes that are covered by Medicare Part D and MAPD plans.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2020 Volume 36 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_19.answers: {"headline_summary":"The current provider manual gives the broader and more up-to-date rule: Medicaid should be billed for a dual-eligible member's drug only when the drug class is excluded from Medicare Part D by law and the drug is Medicaid-reimbursable. The retrieved policy guidance is generally consistent but narrower, because it adds a 2020 operational example that over-the-counter insulin and some over-the-counter products with legend substitutes must be billed to Part D or Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plans instead.","aligned_pairs":[{"provider_manual":"Medicaid should not be billed for a Medicare Part D member's prescription drug unless the drug class is specifically excluded from Part D by law. For dual-eligible members, Medicaid pays only for drugs on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs in those excluded classes. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs.","policy_update":"Policy states that only Medicaid-reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare are covered for dual-eligible members. It gives examples such as select prescription vitamins and over-the-counter drugs. This is a strong match because both sources define when Medicaid may still pay for Part D-excluded drugs."},{"provider_manual":"For full-benefit dual-eligible members, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. Members and prescribers must work with the Part D plan's formulary, exceptions, and appeals process for needed prescriptions. This is the current general rule for routing covered drug classes away from Medicaid.","policy_update":"Policy says Medicaid will no longer cover over-the-counter insulin and some over-the-counter products with legend drug substitutes that are covered by Medicare Part D or Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plans. Those claims must be billed to Part D or Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug plans instead. This is only a partial match because the policy source gives a narrower product-specific example rather than the provider manual's broader current rule."}],"similarities":["Both sources say Medicaid may cover only Medicare-excluded drugs that are also Medicaid-reimbursable for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2020 Volume 36 Number 13]."],"differences":["The provider manual states the broader current rule that Part D-covered drug classes must go through the Part D plan for full-benefit dual eligibles, while the policy source adds a narrower 2020 example for over-the-counter insulin and some over-the-counter products with legend substitutes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2020 Volume 36 Number 13]."],"caveats":"The policy comparison is partial for routing non-excluded or substitute-covered drugs because the retrieved policy source gives a narrower 2020 operational example rather than a general current rule."}

test_id_20.query_content: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

test_id_21.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.answers: {"headline_summary":"The provider manual gives the clearest current rule: pharmacies must bill Medicare first for services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, and they may bill Medicaid only after receiving Medicare payment information. The policy guidance is consistent that Medicaid is the payor of last resort and other liable third parties must be billed first, but it does not state any situation where Medicaid should be billed before Medicare.","aligned_pairs":[{"provider_manual":"For services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid, the pharmacy must bill Medicare first. The pharmacy must bill the appropriate Medicare benefit and may bill Medicaid only after receiving Medicare payment information. The manual also says the pharmacy may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures and must bill by HCPCS or NDC as the Medicare plan requires.","policy_update":"Policy says providers must bill applicable liable third parties before billing Medicaid. It also says Medicaid is always the payor of last resort. This is a strong match, but it is broader than the provider manual because it does not specifically describe Medicare-first billing steps for pharmacies."},{"provider_manual":"Pharmacies must submit claims to a Medicaid member's other coverage under that plan's requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. If claim issues are resolved, the pharmacy may resubmit to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the third-party liability payer. The manual also says bypassing responsible third-party payment is fraudulent billing and requires retaining denial, response, and payment records for six years after Medicaid payment.","policy_update":"Policy says it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, including the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. It also says pharmacies not required to bill the facility must submit transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review program using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs transaction format. This is only a partial match because it is broader and less explicit than the provider manual about billing another payer before Medicaid."}],"similarities":["Both sources say pharmacies must route claims to other liable coverage before billing Medicaid, which places Medicaid after Medicare or other responsible payers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5]."],"differences":["The provider manual gives a specific Medicare-first rule and says Medicaid may be billed only after Medicare payment information is received, while the policy guidance states the payor-of-last-resort principle more broadly and does not describe a Medicaid-before-Medicare exception [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12]."],"caveats":"The policy comparison is partial on operational sequencing because the retrieved policy sources are broader and less pharmacy-specific than the current provider manual guidance."}
